frame_analyses.description: Title slide showing EMIG video tutorial information
Setup view showing laparoscopic trainer with Penrose drain positioned, no instruments visible yet
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Clear view of starting setup with drain and marks visible

frame_analyses.description: Needle driver and other instruments now visible in field, beginning to manipulate suture
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task timing begins - instruments first visible

frame_analyses.description: Needle driver actively grasping and positioning needle with suture
Beginning to position needle for placement through Penrose drain
Actively working to place suture through the marks on the drain
Beginning intracorporeal knot tying after suture placement
Continuing to form the surgeon's knot (double throw)
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Complex loop formation for surgeon's knot

frame_analyses.description: Using scissors to cut suture ends
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Both suture ends being cut inside the trainer

frame_analyses.description: Final view showing completed suture with both ends cut
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task completed with suture properly placed and cut

knot_assessments.notes: This appears to be a running stitch rather than individual knot throws - different from FLS Task 5

drain_assessment.assessment_note: Running suture appears to close the drain slit effectively

technique_summary: This video shows an EMIG running stitch technique rather than FLS Task 5 intracorporeal knot tying. The procedure demonstrates competent suturing skills but does not match the required FLS protocol.
improvement_suggestions: This is the wrong task - should perform FLS Task 5 with surgeon's knot and alternating throws
Need to follow FLS protocol with specific knot sequence
